title: Menu Actions Pattern
summary: The Menu Actions Pattern defines a set of named actions (like a software menu) that you can trigger with short commands during a conversation. Instead of repeating long prompts, you define actions once (e.g., 'write {bullet_point} {num_paragraphs}' to generate text, or just '{bullet_point}' to expand the outline). This turns your prompt into a mini-program with reusable commands. The pattern enables rapid iteration, knowledge sharing across teams, and codifying best-practice prompts into reusable actions.

key_concepts.definition: A prompt pattern that defines named actions triggered by short commands, creating a reusable menu of operations within a conversation.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: This eliminates repetitive prompting. Define actions once, then trigger them with short commands — like a software menu bar for your LLM interaction.

key_concepts.definition: The idea that a well-structured prompt with menu actions functions like a software program — with defined inputs, operations, and outputs.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: This is the culmination of 'everyone can program with prompts' — you're building interactive programs using natural language.

patterns.name: Menu Actions Pattern
patterns.intent: Define reusable actions that can be triggered with short commands, creating a menu of operations for rapid interaction.
patterns.template: Whenever I type {command_format}, you will {action_description}. Whenever I type {other_command}, you will {other_action}.
patterns.example_prompt: Whenever I type 'write <bullet_point> <num_paragraphs>', you will write content for the selected bullet point for the specified number of paragraphs. Whenever I type just a bullet point number, you will expand that bullet point in the outline.
patterns.when_to_use: When you're doing the same tasks repeatedly in a conversation, when building interactive tools with the LLM, or when sharing prompt workflows across a team.

techniques: Define each action with a clear trigger format and description of what it does.
Use placeholders like <bullet_point> and <num_paragraphs> for variable inputs.
The LLM can often infer your intent even with typos (e.g., 'text' instead of 'write').
Share menu action prompts across teams to codify best practices.
Combine with Outline Expansion to create expand + write actions for building large documents.
Menu actions can be baked into root prompts for customer service portals or other applications.

quiz_questions.question: What is the main benefit of the Menu Actions Pattern?
options: A. Making the LLM respond faster
B. Defining reusable actions triggered by short commands, eliminating the need to repeat long prompts
C. Making the LLM more creative
quiz_questions.explanation: Menu Actions let you define operations once and trigger them with short commands (like 'write 3.5.2 2' instead of a full paragraph of instructions), enabling rapid iteration.

quiz_questions.question: In the outline example, what are the two defined menu actions?
options: B. 'write <bullet_point> <num_paragraphs>' to generate text, and '<bullet_point>' alone to expand the outline
quiz_questions.explanation: The instructor defines two actions: typing 'write 3.5.3 2' generates 2 paragraphs of text for bullet 3.5.3, while typing just '3.5.2' expands that bullet point in the outline.

quiz_questions.question: How does the Menu Actions Pattern support team collaboration?
options: A. It doesn't — it's only for individual use
B. Teams can share menu action prompts that codify best-practice wording, enabling consistent and effective LLM use across the organization
C. It automatically shares outputs with team members
D. It connects to team communication tools
quiz_questions.explanation: The instructor emphasizes that organizations can share common menu actions, codifying the best wording for prompts and enabling knowledge sharing and reuse across teams.

raw_examples: Whenever I type 'write <bullet_point> <num_paragraphs>', you will write content for the selected bullet point for the specified number of paragraphs. Whenever I type just a bullet point number, you will expand that bullet point in the outline.
